name: The Long Room Library at Trinity College
description: The gorgeous library at Trinity College is home to remarkable treasures of Irish identity.

name: St. Michan's Mummies
description: An Irish church where an 800-year-old mummy is reaching out of his coffin as if to shake hands.

name: Skellig Michael
description: Perfectly preserved ancient monastery in an impossibly dramatic location on a rocky island in the Atlantic.

name: Dunmore Cave
description: This Irish show cave is said to have been the site of a Viking massacre that claimed 1,000 lives.

name: Irish Sky Garden
description: This floating garden in the sky is straight out of a fairy tale, but the real thing is nothing short of magical.

name: National Leprechaun Museum
description: A museum that cashes in on its infamous little people while teaching you a thing or two about the Irish fey.

name: Saint Audoen's Gate
description: The enchanting archway is the only surviving portal into Dublin's lost medieval wall.

name: Brigit's Celtic Garden
description: The world of the ancient Celtic civilization explained through a bewitching 11 acre garden.

name: Victor's Way Indian Sculpture Park
description: An eccentric garden of sculptures crafted in India decorate an Irish green space dedicated to Alan Turing.

name: The Glen
description: A tiny microvalley etched into the side of Knocknarea Mountain hides another world.

name: Kilmainham Gaol
description: One of Ireland's most infamous prisons for rebels including men, women and children.

name: The Hungry Tree
description: An 80-year-old plane tree is devouring an iron bench at Ireland's oldest law school.

name: Kingship and Sacrifice
description: This permanent collection of preserved bodies taken from Irish bogs is one of the leading authorities on such morbid mummies.

name: Newgrange
description: The ancient tomb acts as a calendar that keeps perfect time.

name: Drombeg Stone Circle
description: One of the most popular megalithic sites in Ireland stands as a humbler version of Stonehenge.

name: Sean's Bar
description: The oldest pub in Ireland may also be the oldest surviving bar in the entire world.

name: Natural History Building, National Museum of Ireland
description: Dublin's "Dead Zoo" serves as a window to Victorian museum design.

name: Abandoned Menlo Castle
description: A moody and picturesque riverside ruin overgrown with vegetation.

name: Dun Aonghasa
description: An Iron Age fort on the edge of a seaside cliff offers a glimpse into an ancient, mystical world.

name: Dublinia
description: Experience what life was like in Ireland during the Viking and medieval ages.

name: The Lost Town of Newtown Jerpoint (Jerpoint Park)
description: Local legend says the remains of Father Christmas lie within the medieval grounds.

name: Statue of Oscar Wilde
description: The comedy and tragedy of Oscar Wilde is preserved in a Dublin park, captured in a sculpture of colorful stone.

name: Kyteler's Inn
description: The oldest inn in Kilkenny was established by the first convicted witch in Ireland.

name: Marsh's Library
description: The oldest public library in Ireland.

name: Oddities of Christ Church Cathedral
description: The mummified heart of a saint lives on in this church—after a brief hiatus when it was stolen.

name: Oratory of the Sacred Heart
description: A nun spent 16 years painting the stunning Celtic mural that covers the interior of this WWI memorial.

name: Jerpoint Abbey
description: This ancient Irish religious site is covered in ancient carvings, many of which have barely aged.

name: Sweny's Pharmacy
description: The 19th-century pharmacy (now a used bookstore) was featured in James Joyce’s "Ulysses," and has changed very little to this day.

name: St. Brigid's Well
description: A sacred well steeped in Pagan roots on the West Coast of Ireland.

name: The Little Museum of Dublin
description: More than 5,000 artifacts donated by the people of the city, plus a room entirely devoted to the band U2.

name: Fiddaun Castle
description: A remarkably intact castle that once was the largest in Ireland.

name: Mound of the Hostages
description: An ancient neolithic tomb that only sees the sun twice a year.

name: Boyeeghter Bay
description: A spectacular hidden beach in Ireland has the dark, disturbing nickname of "Murder Hole Beach."

name: Adare Manor
description: An unusual manor of stunning beauty, born out of a crippling disease.

name: Gap of Dunloe
description: Traveling this mountain pass reveals quaint stone buildings, stunning Irish scenery, and a bridge said to grant wishes.

name: Conolly's Folly
description: Obelisks and stone pineapples in the Irish countryside.

name: The Wonderful Barn
description: A corkscrew-shaped barn in Ireland.

name: The Oscar Wilde House
description: The house where the writer spent his formative years.

name: The Famine Memorial in Dublin
description: The somber sculptures commemorating Irish Famine on the river Liffey in Dublin.

name: Kylemore Abbey
description: A stunning Victorian abbey and walled garden on a lakefront.

name: Seven Churches
description: While only two of the ancient buildings at this site are actually churches this was one of the earliest Irish pilgrimage sites ever.

name: John Kavanagh ('The Gravediggers')
description: This pub built its reputation pouring post-shift pints for employees of the cemetery with which it shares a wall.

name: Glasnevin Cemetery and Museum
description: This is the largest cemetery in Ireland and the first cemetery museum in the world.

name: Freemasons' Hall
description: The Grand Lodge of Ireland is one of the oldest in the world, full of fantastical, elaborately themed rooms.

name: The Butter Museum
description: This Irish museum in a historic 19th century market pays tribute to the glory of butter.

name: The Hole of the Sorrows
description: This singular Irish dolmen is more Frank Lloyd Wright than upright stonehenge.

name: Old Red Iron Bridge
description: Once the longest bridge in Ireland, this rusting span sits abandoned and overrun by nature.

name: Doolin Cave
description: This controversial Irish show cave is home to a massive formation known as the Great Stalactite.

name: Rathcroghan
description: This ancient Irish landscape boasts hundreds of legendary ruins.

name: Sliabh Foy Loop Trail
description: The home of Ireland's last leprechauns is officially protected by the European Union.

name: Hore Abbey
description: The ruins of a medieval monastery named after a type of tree.

name: Murdering Lane and Cutthroat Lane
description: Though they've since been renamed, these two streets hint at Dublin's darker days.

name: The Rock of Dunamase
description: A rocky Irish outcropping with a history of wealth, conflict, and ruin.

name: St. Patrick's Purgatory
description: The world's toughest modern Christian pilgrimage leads directly to the gates of Hell.

name: Fahan Beehive Huts
description: Ireland's most remarkable collection of these curious cone-shaped houses is worth the buzz.

name: Francis Bacon's Preserved Art Studio
description: The chaotic studio of one of the 20th century's most important artists has been preserved down to the dust.

name: Puck's Castle
description: There might not be much left of these Irish ruins, but their linguistic legacy is still going strong.

name: Dublin's Garden of Remembrance
description: Amidst the bustling streets of Dublin, there is a hushed garden that is dedicated to "All those who gave their lives in the cause of Irish Freedom."

name: Kilmacduagh Round Tower
description: The ruins of an 800-year-old monastery boasts the tallest round tower in Ireland.

name: Aungier Street Lidl Archaeology
description: This supermarket offers shoppers a window into the past alongside their groceries.

name: 'Kindred Spirits' Sculpture
description: A tribute to the incredible generosity the Choctaw Nation showed the Irish people during the Great Famine.

name: Ancient Bog Butter
description: The block of aged dairy is probably still edible after 2,300 years in a peat bog.

name: Castleboro House
description: After being destroyed by IRA sympathizers, the ruins of this stately Irish manor have sat vacant for decades.

name: Charleville Castle
description: An 1814 castle that hosts an exhibit of "explorabilia".

name: Great Blasket Island
description: Just a few miles off the western coast of Ireland lies an isle steeped in Irish literary history.

name: The Irish Elk Collection
description: These gigantic skeletons tower over visitors and provide insight into the creatures that once ruled over Ireland.

name: Medb's Cairn
description: This 30-foot pile of rocks may be the grave of a mythological Irish queen.

name: Bridges of Ross
description: This natural sea arch highlights Ireland's rugged coastal beauty.

name: The Joker's Chair
description: This courtly monument celebrates an influential comedian who turned taking the piss out of the powers-that-be into an art.

name: Gallarus Oratory
description: This single room has survived the centuries with almost no deterioration.

name: Writers' Room at The Wicklow Heather
description: A restaurant with an extensive Irish whiskey bar also has several first editions of famous works by Irish authors.

name: Kilmogue Portal Tomb
description: Huge standing stones mark a site of megalithic mystery.

name: Tetrapod Trackway
description: Petrified footprints of one of the very first vertebrates to walk on land.

name: Birr Castle Box Hedge
description: The world's tallest box hedge is found in a delightful garden.

name: Kells Priory
description: These imposing walls and towers look more like the ruins of a fortress than a monastery.

name: Athenry Dominican Priory
description: Ruins battered by centuries of war and disaster offer a wondrous glimpse of medieval Ireland.

name: Ballyedmonduff Wedge Tomb
description: This Bronze Age tomb is the centerpiece of a clearing within a beautiful Irish forest.

name: The Round Tower of Glendalough
description: This ancient Irish tower could easily have stood in for Rapunzel's prison.

name: Lambay Island
description: Wallabies live naturally on this Irish island.

name: Fourknocks Passage Tomb
description: An ancient chamber filled with wonderful rock art and a Neolithic carving of a human face.

name: Seefin Passage Tomb
description: This 5,300-year-old tomb with mysterious carvings crowns an Irish mountain.

name: ‘Why Go Bald’ Sign
description: This neon landmark has been posing this deadpan question to follicly-challenged Dubliners for over 50 years.

name: Benbulben Barite Mine
description: The remains of this deep, dark mine have left behind a haunting rocky warren of tunnels.

name: Knocknakilla Stone Circle
description: Just another ancient, mysterious Irish stone circle.

name: Cathair Crobh Dearg
description: An important pilgrimage site for pagans and early Christians, named for a Celtic goddess-turned-saint.

name: St. Oliver Plunkett's Head
description: The severed head of a 17th-century Irish martyr lies within an intricate golden shrine.

name: Grianán of Aileach
description: You can see three Irish counties from this Neolithic hillside fort, once the seat of the Kingdom of Ailech.

name: Dublin General Post Office
description: The modern birthplace of Irish freedom.

name: The Burren
description: A hugely vast Irish landscape of barren rock that stretches for miles.

name: Kilbeggan Distillery
description: The oldest licensed distillery in Ireland.

name: The Irish National Famine Museum
description: An outstanding and haunting glimpse of the horrors of the Great Famine of Ireland.

name: The Island of Inishmaan
description: Certain traditions prevail on the least-visited island of the Aran Islands.

name: Ashtown Castle
description: After it was swallowed by a Georgian mansion, this medieval castle remained forgotten for centuries.

name: Irish Jewish Museum
description: Catholics make up 84% of Ireland's population but this Dublin museum remembers the small but important Jewish community.

name: Slievemore's deserted stone village
description: Achill's most recently abandoned village dates back to to the Age of Enlightenment.

name: Kinnitty Pyramid
description: This burial pyramid in central Ireland is thousands of miles and thousands of years away from its Egyptian inspiration.

name: MV Plassy
description: A steam trawler with a whiskey-soaked history driven ashore in 1960 resides rusty but still standing on the shoreline.

name: Relics of St. Valentine at  Whitefriar Street Church
description: Some of the saint's blood and bones are said to reside in Dublin.

name: Tullaherin Folk Museum and Round Tower
description: A collection of housewares from recent Irish history adjoined by a tower from ancient Irish history.

name: Dublin Whiskey Fire
description: A devastating booze inferno poured through the streets, claiming several victims who drank it.

name: Cantwell Fada
description: A standing relief of a Norman knight whose posture marks him as a Crusader.

name: King Puck
description: A proud bronze billy stands as an eternal symbol of Ireland's oldest festival, where a goat is made king.

name: The Dragon Gates
description: Fantastic stainless steel dragon gates complete with mesh wings.

name: Knowth
description: This Neolithic passage tomb forms part of a World Heritage Site.

name: Alpacas of Curraghduff Farms
description: Fluffy and friendly alpacas nestled against the border of the beautiful Connemara region.

name: The Calendar Sundial
description: Modern sundial uses ancient methods to tell time and date perfectly.

name: Phil Lynott Statue
description: A statue in the center of Dublin dedicated to one of their favorite sons of rock & roll.

name: Howard Mausoleum Pyramid
description: On the Irish coast, 18 members of the Howard family are eternally resting in an Egyptian pyramid.

name: The Dry Stone Walls of the Aran Islands
description: Mortarless walls that stretch for miles are a lovely solution for creating grazing land in the harsh terrain of the Aran Islands.

name: The Proclamation Sculpture
description: A particularly disconcerting tribute to Irish martyrs.

name: Kilbrittain Whale
description: The skeleton of a whale that beached itself in this Irish town is now on display in their public park.

name: Samuel Beckett Bridge
description: Dublin's most modern, visually spectacular bridge is modeled after Ireland's national emblem.

name: Brownshill Dolmen
description: This European portal tomb has a capstone that weighs around 150 tons.

name: Kilmacurragh House
description: Oliver Cromwell once seized the land under this house, which included the ruins of a medieval abbey that the owner used to build his stately home.

name: Punishment Book at Jameson Distillery
description: The whiskey producer's archives includes a historical list of infractions committed by and associated penalties exacted upon employees.

name: Ye Olde Hurdy Gurdy Museum of Vintage Radio
description: A former fortified tower now houses a collection of classic communications equipment.

name: Leviathan of Parsonstown
description: Behemoth, cannon-like telescope operated by generations of Irish earls.

name: Thoor Ballylee
description: This 14th century tower house was once the home of Irish poet W.B. Yeats.

name: 'Meeting Place'
description: This statue celebrating everyday life in Dublin has a few unsavory nicknames.

name: Darby's Bed
description: A Neolithic passage tomb said to have sheltered two legendary Irish lovers.

name: Landing Site of the First Transatlantic Flight
description: A strange egg in a mucky bog of western Ireland marks the spot of an aviation milestone.

name: Saint Brendan’s Well
description: An ancient holy well said to be used by the patron saint of sailors and travelers, lost in the boggy west of Valentia Island.

name: Kilcoe Castle
description: A stunning medieval castle owned and restored by the famed actor Jeremy Irons.

name: The Hill of Uisneach
description: The symbolic center of Ireland, this summit also offers panoramic views of the countryside.

name: Delta Sensory Gardens
description: Follow lines from a W.B. Yeats poem through interconnecting gardens designed to appeal to each of your senses.

name: Magazine Fort
description: This 18th-century fort surrounded by a moat was used to store gun powder and ammunition, and now sits in the middle of a large park in Dublin.

name: Secret Celtic Tree Cross
description: A massive Celtic cross secretly growing in an Irish forest.

name: Addergoole Titanic Monument
description: This small Irish village suffered the greatest loss of life when the Titanic sank.

name: Barack Obama Plaza
description: This modest plaza in Moneygall, Ireland has cashed in on its distant connection to the U.S. president.

name: O'Shea's Pub
description: An abandoned faux-pub made famous by a Guinness commercial.

name: Tankardstown Engine House
description: Impressive mining ruins overlook a spectacular view of Ireland's Copper Coast.

name: Fr. Pat Noise Memorial Plaque
description: A mysterious plaque to the even more mysterious "Fr. Pat Noise," killed in "suspicious circumstances."

name: Proleek Dolmen
description: A mysterious Neolithic tomb is perched along the edge of a modern golf course.

name: O'Brien's Castle
description: These moss-covered medieval ruins tower atop the smallest of Ireland's Aran Islands.

name: Jumping wall of Kildemock
description: A stone wall that mysteriously moved three feet from its foundation.

name: Baltimore Beacon
description: An oddly conical beacon perched atop beautiful Irish cliffs.

name: Grave of W. B. Yeats
description: This cemetery overlooking the Benbulben rock formation is the final resting place of one of Ireland's most beloved poets.

name: Kilmacduagh Monastic Settlement
description: The ruins of a monastery that dates to the 7th century.

name: Irish National Stud and Gardens
description: See some of the world's greatest racing horses at this idyllic thoroughbred breeding facility and garden complex.

name: Grange Stone Circle Lough Gur
description: The largest stone circle in Ireland.

name: The Autograph Tree
description: This Copper beech tree nestled on a nature reserve is host to a bevy of autographs from Irish literary icons.

name: Lough Boora Discovery Park
description: An Irish peat mine has become a stunning sculpture park.

name: Wolfe Tone Statue
description: An enigmatic monument to the Irish revolutionary who plotted an uprising against British rule.

name: Moor Abbey
description: The remains of a 13th-century Franciscan friary have survived multiple attempts at destruction, most recently in 1921.

name: Charlie Chaplin's Vacation Statue
description: An Irish town has built a statue to a silent film actor simply thanks to the holidays he once spent there.

name: Foynes Flying Boat Museum
description: The world's only flying boat museum honors a small Irish village that sent 20th-century aviation to new heights.

name: Museum of Curiosities
description: A two-storey cabinet of wonders filled with the bizarre, the macabre, and the downright uncanny.

name: Tyrone House
description: The remains of a majestic house that was once stood in the crosshairs of the Irish Republican Army.

name: 'Eire 80' Sign
description: This World War II relic let Allied pilots know they were entering neutral airspace.

name: Rathgall Hillfort
description: Ancient Bronze Age rampart that was used into the middle ages.

name: Clonmacnoise
description: The ruins of a sixth-century monastery sit on the banks of the River Shannon.

name: Fishery Watchtower Museum
description: Originally built to monitor fishing, this Victorian tower now educates visitors about the local salmon industry.

name: The Devil’s Bit
description: It's said this geological anomaly was caused when the Devil took a bite out of the Irish mountainside.

name: Glencree German War Cemetery
description: Ireland's only German military cemetery holds the remains of a spy who was moved there under the cover of darkness.

name: 'Freeflow'
description: Tiny silver and copper fish set inside blue-green lights are like windows to the underwater world.

name: Dunlough Castle
description: The eerie remains of one of Ireland's oldest castles, said to be haunted by the ghost of a woman who brings ill fate.

name: Teampall Bheanáin
description: A diminutive, thousand-year-old church believed to be the smallest in Ireland.

name: Shell Cottage
description: A 300-year-old cottage decorated with tens of thousands of seashells arranged in intricate patterns.

name: Saint Patrick's High Cross
description: This rare and ancient Christian relic is one of Ireland’s oldest freestanding stone crosses.

name: Boole House
description: An important landmark in the history of algebraic logic.

name: Rory Gallagher Corner
description: A guitar mounted on a wall in Temple Bar honors the Irish musician.

name: Inishtrahull
description: Ireland's most northerly island is hauntingly beautiful. Its lighthouse was the last glimpse of home for many Irish emigrants bound for the U.S.

name: Telegraph Field Valentia Island
description: This beautiful site is where the first transatlantic telegraph was sent from.

name: Fort Dunree
description: A retired military stronghold on the rolling green coast of Donegal.

name: Michael Collins Ambush Memorial
description: A gated cross stands on the site where an Irish revolutionary met his end.

name: Vintage Cocktail Club
description: A 1930s-style speakeasy with a secret doorway and one-of-a-kind cocktails.

name: Foot Prints of King George lV
description: Designed to commemorate the king's coronation.

name: Dan Clancy Memorial Chair
description: A teddy bear sits on a chair watching the sunset from the mountaintop—just as the late Dan Clancy had done since childhood.

name: Dún na mBó
description: When the tide is high, you can see the Atlantic pushing up through a deep, jagged hole in the rock.

name: Cork City Gaol
description: This 19th-century prison once held Irish revolutionary Constance Markievicz, writer Frank O’Connor, and a nine-year-old pickpocket.

name: Blarney Castle Horse Graveyard
description: Horse cemetery on the grounds of Blarney Castle.

name: Arbour Hill Cemetery
description: The burial place of 14 of the executed leaders of the Easter Rising.

name: SS Crete Boom
description: The remains of a concrete ship sit on a sandbank between Ballina Quay and Belleek Woods.

name: The Four-Faced Liar
description: On the clock tower at St. Anne's Church in Cork, you never know which face is telling the truth.

name: Ringfort Relic
description: An unassuming historical marker for an iron age fort leads you to an oddly adorned cemetery for unbaptized children.

name: 'The Colleen Bawn' Bust
description: A monument to a 19th-century Irish woman whose murder inspired several literary works.

name: Hook Lighthouse
description: One of the oldest lighthouses in the world, this structure was built more than 800 years ago.

name: Battle of the Boyne Visitor Centre
description: This center tells the story of one of the most famous battles in Irish history.

name: Creevykeel Court Tomb
description: One of Ireland's best-preserved chambered cairns can be found next to a wishing tree.

name: Killeshin Church
description: An elaborate Romanesque archway, built right before the British occupation of Ireland.

name: Brennanstown Standing Stone
description: One of Ireland's lesser-known megalithic structures.

name: The Great South Wall
description: The Dublin sea wall draws walkers, photographers, and fishing enthusiasts.

name: Ardnacrusha Power Station
description: At the time of its construction, this hydroelectric power plant was the largest in the world.

name: Loughcrew Cairns
description: These Neolithic burial mounds and a still-intact passage tomb may have solar calendar significance on the spring and autumn equinoxes.

name: Ringfinnan Garden of Remembrance
description: This memorial was created by an Irish nurse who lived in New York City during 9/11.

name: National Print Museum
description: Collection of still-functioning printing presses from the 18th to 20th centuries.

name: Ireland's Oldest Postbox
description: Inside Cork's Kent station stands its country's longest-serving postbox.

name: Costello Memorial Chapel
description: The smallest chapel in Europe is a moving testament to the love between a husband and wife.

name: Dalkey Island
description: A tiny island off the coast of Dublin was once a pilgrimage site for the sick and the suffering.

name: The Central Shop
description: Not actually a shop, but one fan's passionate show of support for a rival county's Gaelic football team.

name: St. Mary's Church
description: For fans of the stained glass art of Irish illustrator Harry Clarke, this church is the place to be.

name: Clocha na hÉireann (Stones of Ireland)
description: A map made up of 32 different materials, representing Ireland's 32 counties.

name: Farren’s Bar
description: Ireland’s northernmost pub.

name: Doagh Famine Village
description: Once part of a living village, these buildings now house exhibits showcasing historical aspects of life in Ireland.

name: Gallen Priory
description: Once an early Christian burial site, it's believed the location was home to around 200 gravestones.

name: Spanish Arch
description: Despite the name, this 16th-century structure in Galway wasn't built by the Spanish.

name: Carlow Military Museum
description: Curated by a small staff of veterans, the Carlow Military Museum valiantly provides a personal perspective on over 800 years of Irish history.

name: Dublin Mile Markers
description: Built into the wall of this pub is a unique marker detailing the distance to Dublin.

name: Irish Workhouse Centre
description: A well-preserved, 19th-century Irish workhouse recounts a grim time in the country’s history.

name: Me Little Brudder Memorial
description: A small memorial commemorates a man who died at Kilkee Cliffs.

name: Carlow Castle
description: The remains of a 13th-century Norman fortress, largely destroyed by an explosive renovation.

name: Dursey Island Cable Car
description: Ireland's only cable car once carried livestock across a treacherous strait.

name: Cabra Park Urban Gallery
description: Explore Dublin's laneways full of diverse street art and graffiti.

name: Fanny O’Deas
description: One of Ireland’s oldest family-run pubs, with a unique feature.

name: Mamore Gap
description: This scenic road offers breathtaking mountain pass views and a quirky “magic road” illusion.

name: Broom Bridge
description: The graffiti that birthed modern algebra.

name: Temple Hill Cemetery in Ballintemple
description: Both Catholic and Protestant graves dot this 17th-century burial ground.

name: Dalymount Park Murals
description: Outside an Irish football stadium are murals commemorating history and local heroes.

name: Spire of Lloyd
description: “Ireland’s only inland lighthouse” is not actually a lighthouse at all.

name: Fungie the Dingle Dolphin
description: A monument to a beloved aquatic folk hero in the Irish town of Dingle.

name: Bonavox
description: This is the place where U2’s lead singer got his nickname.

name: Che Guevara Mural
description: A serendipitous meeting between a South American revolutionary and a local barman resulted in an iconic image of 20th-century art.

name: Flight of the Earls Memorial
description: Commemorating the end of independent Gaelic Ireland.

name: Anti-War Memorial
description: A cynical face-off exposing the cost of war.

name: St. Aengus Church
description: This Irish church has a striking circular design inspired by the ancient Grianán of Aileach.

name: William Sealy Gosset Plaque
description: Honoring the Guinness brewer whose quest for the perfect pint led to the creation of the t-test, a cornerstone of modern statistics.

name: Áras na nGael
description: At this cozy bar tucked down a hidden Galway backroad, Guinness makes the Gaelic come out.

name: Rindoon
description: Wander among the abandoned ruins of this once-powerful medieval town.

name: Táin Bó Cúailnge Mosaic
description: This colorful and fiery mural depicts the Irish folk story of the beloved hero Cuchulain.

name: Dublin Writers Museum
description: This museum is dedicated to the most famous Irish writers in history.

name: Conger Bread
description: After a traumatic encounter with a six-foot eel, one Galway baker started making a daily loaf of sourdough in its shape.
